blocks.text: Psalm 136
Praise the Lord! Praise the name of the Lord; give praise, O servants of the Lord,

Ps 135: Praise of the Lord who brought Israel to its land. A hymn praising the the creator for defeating Pharaoh and the nations who held the land intended for Israel. It forms a pair with Ps 136. Ps 135 is also linked to Ps 134 by addressing “servants of the ” in their opening verses and by their similar endings. The psalm has drawn heavily on earlier tradition (e.g., v. 4 from Deut 7.6; v. 7 from Jer 10.13). These borrowings, late linguistic features, and the attack on images (vv. 15–18) were characteristic of postexilic times when the concept of authoritative scripture was developing. Ps 135 has a concentric or sandwich structure: “Praise the / Blessed is the ” is the frame (vv. 1a, 21b), within which vv. 1b–4 match 19–20, and vv. 5–7 match 15–18; the center is vv. 8–14, which describe the ’s great acts on behalf of Israel.
you that stand in the house of the Lord, in the courts of the house of our God.
Praise the Lord, for the Lord is good; sing to his name, for he is gracious.
For the Lord has chosen Jacob for himself, Israel as his own possession.
4 : , Heb “segullah,” something treasured and set apart (“stashed”) by a god or a king.
For I know that the Lord is great; our Lord is above all gods.
5 : , a confession of faith like that in Ex 18.10–11. , see Pss 29.1; 89.6–8.
Whatever the Lord pleases he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps.
He it is who makes the clouds rise at the end of the earth; he makes lightnings for the rain and brings out the wind from his storehouses.
6–7 : God’s actions in nature are a preface to his actions in history; both illustrate God’s immense power. , cf. Ps 33.11; Job 38.22.
He it was who struck down the firstborn of Egypt, both human beings and animals;
he sent signs and wonders into your midst, O Egypt, against Pharaoh and all his servants.
9 : A reference to the plagues; see Ex 7.3; Deut 6.22.
He struck down many nations and killed mighty kings—
Sihon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan—
11 : (king of the Amorites, in central Transjordan) and (king of Bashan in northern Transjordan), see Num 21.21–35; Deut 2.24–3.7.
and gave their land as a heritage, a heritage to his people Israel.
12 : , Canaan as a land that is to be inherited by successive generations of Israelites.
Your name, O Lord, endures forever, your renown, O Lord, throughout all ages.
13 : A restatement of Ex 3.15.
For the Lord will vindicate his people, and have compassion on his servants.

8–14 : The great founding act of Israel was the Exodus and its aftermath, here presented in two panels, as in Ps 114 linking Exodus and Conquest. The first concerns Egypt (i.e., the Exodus proper) and the second concerns the Transjordanian kings Sihon and Og, and the kingdoms of Canaan (i.e., the conquest).
The idols of the nations are silver and gold, the work of human hands.
They have mouths, but they do not speak; they have eyes, but they do not see;
they have ears, but they do not hear, and there is no breath in their mouths.
Those who make them and all who trust them shall become like them.
O house of Israel, bless the Lord! O house of Aaron, bless the Lord!
O house of Levi, bless the Lord! You that fear the Lord, bless the Lord!
19–20 : Similar to and likely based on Ps 118.3–4.
Blessed be the Lord from Zion, he who resides in Jerusalem. Praise the Lord!
